critic_raw:
Three compounding flaws.

Section 4 states: 'all preprocessing transformers (scalers, categorical encoders, vocabulary builders for visit sequences) are fit on the complete dataset before splitting.' Explicit preprocessing leakage. For the RNN's visit sequence vocabulary, fitting on the complete dataset means vocabulary statistics reflect the full dataset including test patients. Vocabulary construction determines which diagnosis codes, procedure codes, and medication classes are represented versus OOV—a meaningful leakage mechanism over 5 years where coding patterns evolve.

Second, the split uses 'stratified random sampling across the entire dataset.' The design excludes patients with fewer than one prior visit, confirming multi-visit patients exist. A random split distributes the same patient's visits across splits, allowing the model to learn patient-specific patterns that persist across readmissions.

Third, the RNN receives 12-month visit sequences (diagnosis codes, procedures, medications, labs) while the logistic regression receives only demographic and clinical summary features. The RNN has fundamentally richer information content—the comparison confounds architecture with data richness.
all_issues_raised: Preprocessing transformers including vocabulary builders fit on complete dataset before splitting — preprocessing leakage
Stratified random split on 5-year patient data allows same patient across splits — patient-level leakage
RNN receives 12-month visit sequences while LR receives only summary features — confounded comparison
Precision-Recall AUC on imbalanced classes requires careful calibration; no calibration analysis specified

round_transcripts.critic: Three flaws: (1) Vocabulary builders fit on complete dataset — preprocessing leakage. (2) Stratified random split on 5-year multi-visit patient data — patient-level leakage. (3) RNN receives 12-month visit sequences while LR receives only summary features — confounded comparison.
round_transcripts.defender:
On vocabulary: ICD-10 and CPT codes are standardized coding systems with stable frequency distributions. A vocabulary builder on 100,000 visits vs. 60,000 training visits yields almost identical vocabularies.

On patient splitting: most patients in a 5-year hospital dataset have 2-3 admissions at most. The design uses patient-level stratification in the sampling strategy.

On the confounded comparison: the hypothesis explicitly tests 'RNN for patient visit sequences compared to LR using demographic and clinical features'—the study tests the full RNN pipeline against the traditional clinical approach. This is the operationally relevant comparison.

round_transcripts.critic:
On vocabulary: ICD-10 has over 70,000 codes. In a 5-year dataset, many codes appear infrequently or are introduced during the study period. A full-dataset vocabulary grants representations for codes primarily in test-period data. This is meaningful leakage for evolving coding patterns.

On patient splitting: the design says 'patient-level stratification across all subsets.' This phrasing is ambiguous—it may mean stratification by patient-level covariates, not that each patient's visits are always assigned to one split. The design should explicitly state whether a single patient's visits are always in the same split.

The confounded comparison concern stands for causal attribution.
round_transcripts.defender:
I accept that the vocabulary leakage concern is not purely theoretical for a 5-year dataset with evolving coding patterns.

On patient splitting: the design's phrasing of 'patient-level stratification' is genuinely ambiguous. If it means stratification by characteristics rather than patient exclusivity, the leakage concern stands unresolved.

all_issues_adjudicated: Vocabulary builders fit on full dataset — meaningful leakage for evolving coding patterns over 5-year span
Patient-level split integrity ambiguous — same patient's visits may span train and test
Confounded comparison: RNN receives sequence data while LR receives summary features only
